title: Ubuntu Terminal Basics
description: Master essential Ubuntu terminal commands for file management, system operations, and text processing.

questions.prompt: What does the -l flag do in the ls command?

options.label: List files in long format
options.explanation: The -l flag displays detailed information including permissions, owner, size, and modification date for each file.

questions.explanation: The ls -l command shows files in long format with detailed information.

questions.prompt: What does the -r flag do in grep?

options.label: Search recursively through directories
options.explanation: The -r flag makes grep search inside directories recursively, descending into subdirectories to find matches.

questions.explanation: grep searches for matching text. With -r, it searches recursively through directories.

options.label: Creates parent directories as needed
options.explanation: The -p flag creates all necessary parent directories in the path, preventing errors if they don't exist.

questions.explanation: mkdir -p creates nested directories in one command without errors.

questions.prompt: What permissions does chmod 755 give to a file?

options.label: Owner: rwx, Group: rx, Others: rx
options.explanation: 755 means: owner gets read/write/execute (7), group gets read/execute (5), others get read/execute (5).

options.label: Owner: rw, Group: rw, Others: rw
Owner: rwx, Group: rwx, Others: rwx
Owner: rx, Group: rx, Others: rx

questions.explanation: chmod 755 is commonly used for executable scripts and directories.

options.label: Disk space usage in human-readable format
options.explanation: The -h flag shows disk space in GB/MB instead of bytes, making it easier to read.

questions.explanation: df -h shows how much disk space is used and available on all mounted filesystems.

questions.prompt: Type the command to display the current working directory.
questions.explanation: pwd (print working directory) shows the full path of your current location in the filesystem.

questions.prompt: Type the command to clear the terminal screen.
questions.explanation: clear removes all previous output from the terminal screen, giving you a clean workspace.

questions.prompt: What does the cat command do?

options.label: Displays the contents of a file
options.explanation: cat (concatenate) reads files and prints their content to the terminal.

questions.explanation: cat is one of the most basic commands for viewing file contents.

options.label: Removes files and directories recursively without prompting
options.explanation: The -r flag removes directories recursively, and -f forces deletion without confirmation. Use with extreme caution!

questions.explanation: rm -rf is powerful and dangerous - it permanently deletes without asking for confirmation.

questions.prompt: Type the command to display running processes in real-time.
questions.explanation: top shows a dynamic, real-time view of running processes, CPU usage, and memory consumption.

questions.prompt: What does the pipe symbol | do in commands?

options.label: Sends output of one command as input to another
options.explanation: The pipe | connects commands, allowing you to chain operations together.

options.label: Separates multiple commands to run in parallel

questions.explanation: Pipes are fundamental for combining simple commands into powerful workflows.

questions.prompt: What does the sudo command do?

options.label: Executes a command with superuser privileges
options.explanation: sudo (superuser do) allows authorized users to run commands as root or another user.

questions.explanation: sudo is essential for administrative tasks that require elevated permissions.

questions.prompt: Type the command to view the manual page for a command.
questions.explanation: man (manual) displays detailed documentation for commands, including all available options and examples.

options.label: Finds all .txt files in current directory and subdirectories
options.explanation: find searches recursively from the current directory (.) for files matching the pattern *.txt.

questions.explanation: find is powerful for locating files based on various criteria like name, size, or modification date.

options.label: Makes the script executable
options.explanation: The +x flag adds execute permission, allowing the script to be run as a program.

questions.explanation: You must make shell scripts executable before you can run them with ./script.sh.
